ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Aloo Gobi exists; mapped to the closest vegetable-based dish concept. FoodOn FOODON_03311069 covers potato-based dishes as the nearest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated Aloo Gobi entry. A00GJ (Potato dishes) is used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor; cauliflower component is not separately coded at this level.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for Aloo Gobi or equivalent Indian vegetable curry dish. Left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Aloo Gobi in Singapore is approximately 250 g, representing one shared side dish or a single-person main accompaniment served with rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Aloo Gobi (Potato and Cauliflower Curry) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) provides values for potato-based vegetable preparations and cauliflower dishes. Per-100g values for Aloo Gobi are derived by adapting the ICMR-NIN cooked potato curry and cooked cauliflower sabzi entries, weighted approximately 55% potato and 45% cauliflower with standard spice and oil additions (approximately 5 g oil per 100 g dish), consistent with typical North Indian home-style and restaurant preparation. Energy and macros are cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked potato (FDC ID 170026) and cooked cauliflower (FDC ID 169986) to confirm plausibility.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Potato (boiled/cooked) as analogous anchor for Aloo Gobi
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Aloo Gobi exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated using boiled/cooked potato (GI ~65, medium, per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables) as the primary carbohydrate driver (~55% of dish weight). Cauliflower has a negligible GI contribution. The addition of fat (oil) and fibre from cauliflower and spices is expected to modestly lower the effective GI relative to plain boiled potato; the estimate of 65 is therefore a conservative upper-bound for the dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 33.0) / 100 = 21.5, using 250 g serving size and 13.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from added salt and spice blends
hypertension.guidance: A 250 g restaurant serving of Aloo Gobi contributes approximately 775 mg sodium, representing about one-third of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index driven by potato starch content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 65 and a glycemic load of 21.5 per standard 250 g serving, Aloo Gobi produces a moderate postprandial glucose response; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should pair it with a low-GI food such as dal or a high-fibre grain and limit portion to 150–180 g to manage glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, cholesterol-free, and rich in potassium and vitamin C
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Aloo Gobi is cholesterol-free with only 0.5 g saturated fat per 100 g and provides potassium (~380 mg/100 g) and vitamin C (~22 mg/100 g), which support vascular health; the primary cardiovascular concern is sodium from restaurant preparation, which should be moderated.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Aloo Gobi is a classic North Indian dry or semi-dry vegetable dish made from potatoes (aloo) and cauliflower (gobi) cooked together with a fragrant blend of spices including cumin, turmeric, coriander, garam masala, ginger, and garlic. The dish is typically prepared by tempering whole spices in oil, then sautéing onions and tomatoes before adding parboiled or raw potato and cauliflower florets, which are cooked until tender and coated in the spice mixture. The result is a golden-yellow, lightly caramelised dish with a dry or slightly saucy texture, earthy and warming flavour, and a mildly spiced aroma. Aloo Gobi is a staple of North Indian home cooking and is widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore and globally. It is naturally vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free, making it suitable for a broad range of dietary preferences. Nutritionally, a 100 g serving provides approximately 97 kcal, 13.2 g carbohydrate, 2.4 g protein, and 4.2 g fat, with meaningful dietary fibre (2.3 g), vitamin C (22 mg), potassium (380 mg), and folate (28 mcg DFE). The dish has a medium glycemic index (~65) due to its potato content, and individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should moderate portion size. Sodium content in restaurant versions can be elevated (~310 mg/100 g), which is relevant for hypertensive patients. The low saturated fat and absence of cholesterol make it a heart-friendly choice when sodium is controlled.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Aloo Gobi?
Is Aloo Gobi good for diabetics?
What spices are in Aloo Gobi?
Can I eat Aloo Gobi if I have high blood pressure?
meal_context: served with roti, naan, or rice

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) using weighted composition of cooked potato and cauliflower with standard oil addition, cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. No single exact ICMR-NIN entry for Aloo Gobi as a composite dish exists; adaptation is well-grounded but not an exact match. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database using boiled potato as the primary carbohydrate driver. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values represent a typical North Indian restaurant-style Aloo Gobi preparation. Actual values will vary by recipe, oil quantity, and restaurant. 2. Sodium content (310 mg/100 g) reflects restaurant preparation with added salt; home-cooked versions may be significantly lower. 3. GI of 65 is a conservative analogous estimate; the actual GI of Aloo Gobi may be slightly lower due to fat and fibre moderating starch digestion. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients, so B12 is naturally zero). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — no animal or fortified ingredients present). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — entirely plant-based dish). trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats used in traditional preparation). 5. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are approximate matches; no exact ontology term for Aloo Gobi exists in current public releases. 6. Singapore restaurant price range: SGD 10.88–17.90 across 13 field survey-listed restaurants (source: field survey data, preserved from input for commercial context only, not used as nutritional evidence). 7. This record is suitable for RAG and LLM training use for Indian cuisine, vegetarian food, and South/Southeast Asian dietary contexts.
